coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots derived from lily bulbs for copper ion sensing and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Gu, Dan
Zhang, Pingbo
Zhang, Lei
Liu, Hao
Pu, Zhanbin
Shang, Shaoming
coredata.dc:description: A straightforward, rapid, and environmentally-friendly technique was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the use of lily bulbs (LB) as raw materials. The as-synthesized LB-CDs showed high stability in aqueous solution and high fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of 17.6%. The LB-CDs were then used as a fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ ion detection based on the Cu2+-ion-stimulated fluorescence quenching of LB-CDs. The synthesized LB-CDs demonstrated exceptional selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+ ions, with a limit of detection of 12.8 nM and a linear detection range of 0.05–2.0 μM. The synthesized LB-CDs were also applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples. Moreover, the LB-CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity, which is suitable for fluorescence-based analysis and cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lily bulbs
Cu2+
Cell imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of LB-CDs and their application to Cu2+ detection and cell imaging.
(a) Transmission electron microscopy images of LB-CDs. (b) Particle size distribution of LB-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of LB-CDs. The XPS (b), C1s (c), N1s (d), O1s (e), and P2p (f) spectra of the as-attained LB-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra of the aqueous solution of LB-CDs (insets show the photographs of LB-CDs under visible light (left) and UV light (right)). (b) PL spectra of LB-CDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 nm to 480 nm.

(a) Effects of pH on the photoluminescence (PL) intensity of LB-CDs. (b) Effects of ionic strengths adjusted by various concentrations of NaCl on the PL intensity of LB-CDs. (c) Changes of the PL intensity of LB-CDs under irradiation by UV lamp with different time intervals. (d) Fluorescent intensity of LB-CDs upon the addition of various metal ions in HAc–NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.0; 10 mmol L−1). The concentration of every metal ion is 50 μmol L−1. F0 and F denote the fluorescent intensity of LB-CDs at λ em of 448 nm excited at 370 nm in the inexistence and existence of metal ions, respectively.

UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+ (red line), LB-CDs (black line), and LB-CDs + Cu2+ (blue line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Fluorescent spectra of LB-CDs in the existence of various amounts of Cu2+ ranging from 0 μmol L−1 to 50.0 μmol L−1 in HAc–NaAc buffer at pH 4. Inset shows the dependence of (F0 − F)/F0 on Cu2+ concentrations within the range of 0–50 μM. (b) The linear dependence of (F0 − F)/F0 on Cu2+ concentrations within the range of 0.05–2.0 μM.
Fluorescence microscope pictures of A549 cells incubated with LB-CDs for 24 h under (a) 405 nm excitation and (b) bright field, (c) The merged image of a and b.
Comparisons of the quantum yield of the LB-CDs with those of other CDs derived from natural materials.
Comparison of different chemosensor for Cu2+sensing.
Detection of Cu2+ in tap water and lake water.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots derived from lily bulbs for copper ion sensing and cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids
School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward, rapid, and environmentally-friendly technique was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) with the use of lily bulbs (LB) as raw materials. The as-synthesized LB-CDs showed high stability in aqueous solution and high fluorescence with a quantum yield (QY) of 17.6%. The LB-CDs were then used as a fluorescence sensor for Cu2+ ion detection based on the Cu2+-ion-stimulated fluorescence quenching of LB-CDs. The synthesized LB-CDs demonstrated exceptional selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+ ions, with a limit of detection of 12.8 nM and a linear detection range of 0.05–2.0 μM. The synthesized LB-CDs were also applied for the detection of Cu2+ ions in real water samples. Moreover, the LB-CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity, which is suitable for fluorescence-based analysis and cell imaging.

Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots were synthesized by a straightforward, fast and affordable microwave method, using lily bulbs as carbon source. These nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots can be used for Cu2+ detection and cell imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is considered an important transition metal for human beings and has an important role in biological systems. Copper is mostly found in natural and environmental water, for instance, river water, lake water, tap water, seawater, and drinking water [1,2]. Nevertheless, copper is highly toxic at high concentrations with long-term contact with the human body. Excessive copper concentration can cause numerous neurodegenerative ailments, such as Alzheimer's disease, prion disease, and Wilson's disease [3]. In accordance with the US Environmental Protection Agency, the maximum limit of Cu2+ in drinking water should be 20 μM [4]. So, monitoring the environmental copper ions concentrations with elevated sensitivity and selectivity is important. Although different analytical approaches, including electrochemical sensors, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, potentiometric sensors, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and atomic fluorescence spectroscopy [5–9], can be applied to detect copper ions with high sensitivity, their applications are limited by high costs, time-consuming process, intricate specimen development, and improper real-time inspection.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted the interest of researchers because of their promising uses in different fields ranging from drug delivery [10], sensing [11–13], optoelectronic apparatus [14] to imaging [15]. In comparison with traditional organic fluorescent dyes and heavy-metal-based quantum dots, CDs have been emphasized because of their simple synthesis, exceptional water solubility, low toxicity, elevated photostability, affordability, and extraordinary biocompatibility [16–18]. In particular, on the basis of their powerful and adjustable photoluminescence (PL) characteristics, CDs are applied to detect heavy metal ions [19]. Currently, some natural items that contain nitrogen, for instance, papaya [20], winter melon [21], and konjac flour [22], were used as precursors for the fabrication of nitrogen-doped CDs with the application of hydrothermal treatment. However, the nitrogen-doped CDs developed from these affordable and abundant precursors did not attain high quantum yield (QY) (typically <10%), resulting in problems during practical use. It has been reported that heteroatom-doped carbon nanomaterials are motivating high research preferences that are capable of productively tuning their inherent properties, including optical features and local and surface chemical properties [23,24]. Thus, the search for a method for the synthesis of heteroatom-doped and high PL CDs is recommended. Li et al. proposed a rapid and effective microwave-assisted technique for the purpose of synthesizing phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped CDs from N-phosphonomethyl aminodiacetic acid and ethylenediamine for fluorescent cell imaging [25]. Gong's group synthesized phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots with brilliant green PL by mixing glucose, 1, 2-ethylenediamine, and concentrated phosphoric acid (H3PO4) [26]. Nevertheless, all these mechanisms take a long time and are not eco-friendly, confining their broad use. Accordingly, establishing a straightforward and environmentally-friendly technique to obtaining highly fluorescent doped CDs remains a challenging task.

Most natural substances have intricate components, and CDs developed from these substances normally have different surface groups with unique properties. To the best of our knowledge, the synthesis of phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped CDs from natural sources has never been reported. Therefore, the development of rapid and affordable approaches for the synthesis of nontoxic phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped fluorescent CDs from natural sources still continues to be a challenge. Lily bulbs (LB) have abundant carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, phosphorus, and hydrogen components because of the presence of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and amino acids. Accordingly, we expect that this natural substance should have a potential for the synthesis of phosphorus and nitrogen co-doped CDs.

In this study, we have established an exceptionally green and affordable strategy to fabricate elevated luminescent LB-CDs through the application of one-pot microwave-assisted treatment of LB. The synthetic LB-CDs exhibit elevated fluorescence with QY of 17.6%, with extraordinary selectivity and sensitivity to Cu2+. The detection limit for Cu2+ is as low as 12.8 nM, which shows that this substance is an appropriate candidate for the ecological detection of Cu2+. The findings from the calculation of Cu2+ in river water provide evidence for our supposition and indicate its promising use in practical applications. Meanwhile, LB-CDs were applied to cell imaging, which indicates their capability in the biomedical fields.
The materials used, the characterization methods, and the cell toxicity tests are shown in the supporting information (SI).

LB-CDs were synthesized through a microwave-assisted approach that used LB as carbon precursor. Briefly, 12 g of newly cut LB was shifted to a domestic 800 W microwave oven, followed by heating for 6 min in an autoclave. Deionized water (20 mL) was added to the reaction mix after cooling down to room temperature in a natural manner. The suspension was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 15 min), followed by passing through a 0.22 μm syringe-driven filter and dialysis against deionized water with the help of a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 48 h. Finally, a translucent brown aqueous solution that contains LB-CDs was lyophilized to obtain dry LB-CDs.

The determination of the QY of LB-CDs was made in accordance with a developed mechanism. Briefly, quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid (QY = 54%) was selected as the standard. Absorbances in the 1.0 cm fluorescence curette were maintained under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm to minimize the reabsorption effects. The QY of LB-CDs was calculated with the application of the following equation:
Q = Q R × (I/I R) × (A R/A) × (n/n R)2,
where Q indicates the QY, I indicates the calculated integrated fluorescent emission intensity, n indicates the refractive index of the solvent, and A denotes the optical intensity at excitation wavelength. The subscript R refers to the standard.

The fluorescent detection of Cu2+ ions was conducted at room temperature in distilled water. In this specific test, an appropriate quantity of Cu2+ ions was added into 2 mL of acetic acid–sodium acetate buffer solution (pH = 4) that contained LB-CDs. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after the 3 min reaction at room temperature. Confirmation of the selectivity of Cu2+ ions was conducted through the addition of various stock solutions of metal ions rather than Cu2+ ions in the same manner.

Human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells were cultured in high-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium that contained 100 μg mL−1 penicillin, 100 μg mL−1 streptomycin, and 10% fetal bovine serum. In the duration of propagation, human lung adenocarcinoma A549 cells were dispersed in 24 duplicate wells and incubated at a temperature of 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator for 24 h. Subsequently, the culture source was substituted with a fresh source that contained LB-CDs at the density of 1 mg mL−1, followed by the incubation of A549 cells for 2 h. The cells were washed three times using phosphate-buffered saline and imaged with an inverted Olympus IX51 fluorescence microscope.

As presented in Fig. 1 , LB-CDs were prepared using LB as carbon precursor through a plain-microwave-assisted treatment. The size allocation and morphology of the attained LB-CDs were characterized using TEM. As indicated by the TEM images, LB-CDs are homogeneous, with particles spread in a spherical shape (Fig. 2 a). The diameter of the obtained LB-CDs ranged between 1.34 nm and 5.23 nm, with the median size of 3.15 nm (Fig. 2b). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained to identify the functional groups of LB-CDs, as presented in Fig. 3 a. The distinctive peak at 3277 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of the –OH and H2N– groups, which ensure the dispersibility and constancy of LB-CDs in water. The peak at 2921 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–H group. The peak situated at 1583 cm−1 stems from the N–H flexing vibrations. The peak centered at 1380 cm−1 is attributed to the C–N stretching vibrations. The peak located at 1251 cm−1 stems from the C–P stretching vibrations. The peak at 1027 cm−1 is ascribed to the C–O stretching vibrations. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was also conducted to examine the surface functional groups and element states of LB-CDs. The existence of C, N, O, and P in the synthesized LB-CDs is confirmed by XPS (Fig. 3b). The C1s XPS spectrum exhibits four kinds of carbon atoms (Fig. 3c), as follows: C=C/C–C having a binding energy of 284.5 eV; C–P having a binding energy of 285.2 eV; C=O having a binding energy of 287.9 eV; and C–N having a binding energy of 286.3 eV. The N1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 399.8 and 401.5 eV (Fig. 3d) that are attributed to the C–N–C and surface H2N– groups, respectively. The O1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.8 and 532.8 eV (Fig. 3e) that are ascribed to the C–N–O/P–O and C–OH groups, respectively. The P2p spectrum (Fig. 3f) sheds light on the existence of P–O (133.1eV), P–C (134.0 eV), and P2p3/2 (133.4ev). FTIR and XPS characterizations indicate that nitrogen and phosphorus have been successfully doped into LB-CDs.

We conducted additional study of the fluorescent properties of LB-CDs under various conditions. We also investigated the effect of pH on the PL intensity of LB-CDs (Fig. 5 a). The PL intensity of LB-CDs increased as the pH ranged from 2 to 4 and continued to increase from 4 to 10. Subsequently, the PL intensity decreased stepwise with the pH ranging from 10 to 13. The effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescent intensity of LB-CDs is presented in Fig. 5b. The fluorescent intensities continue to be steady with the increase in NaCl concentration and are expected to benefit the application in the existence of miscellaneous salt concentrations in actual uses. Fig. 5c sheds light on the fact that the PL intensity of LB-CDs continues to be stable under constant irradiation by UV lamp for numbers of hours, indicating the fascinating photostability of the fluorescent probe. As indicated by the findings, LB-CDs possess an elevated potential for sense applications under physiological states.

Notably, we discovered that Cu2+ is capable of apparently quenching the fluorescence of LB-CDs, indicating that LB-CDs can be potentially utilized as a Cu2+ sensor. The PL intensity of LB-CDs was investigated in the existence of an array of metal ions, including Fe2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Sr2+, and Ag+, at equal concentrations (50 μmol L−1) to evaluate the selectivity of the current sensing system. The findings presented in Fig. 5d indicate that only Cu2+ stimulates observable quenching, whereas the other metal ions exhibit negligible quenching effects. Fig. S1 shows the consequences of the interference tests. In the same manner, the fluorescent intensities of LB-CDs do not have any apparent variation following its mixture with any one of the interference metal ions. Nevertheless, the considerable fluorescence quenching is observed upon introduction of Cu2+ in all of the experimental solutions. As indicated by the consequences, these metal ions have either no or slight quenching effect on the detection of Cu2+. These consequences indicate exceptional selectivity of the current LB-CDs with respect to sensing of Cu2+ because of the forceful appetency of Cu2+ compared with any other metal ion with respect to the carboxylic, hydroxyl, and amino groups on the surface of LB-CDs. The as-developed LB-CDs with miscellaneous surface functional groups are also likely to exhibit exceptional appetency with Cu2+ for forming steady chelate complexes. The developed LB-CDs + Cu2+ complexes are expected to promote charge shift and restrain the radiative recombination of excitons, resulting in distinct fluorescent quenching effects.

Fluorescent lifetime calculations were conducted to obtain additional insights into the fluorescent quenching process of LB-CDs by Cu2+. The fluorescent lifetime of blank LB-CDs amounts to 4.29 and 4.26 ns with the availability of Cu2+. As indicated by these findings, the fluorescent quenching process of LB-CDs by Cu2+ appears to be a static quenching mechanism. Thus, the elevated selectivity of LB-CDs is because of the more forceful appetency and quicker chelating kinetics of Cu2+ than other metal ions with respect to the hydroxyl, carboxylic, and amino groups on the surface of LB-CDs. The as-developed LB-CDs with a variety of surface functional groups are also likely to exhibit exceptional appetency with Cu2+ for forming steady chelate complexes. The UV–vis absorption spectra of LB-CDs and LB-CDs + Cu2+ were recorded to comprehend the bond between LB-CDs and Cu2+. As evidently shown in Fig. 6 , the absorption peak of LB-CDs is situated at 280 nm. Nevertheless, the absorption peak of LB-CDs exhibited a remarkable disappearance after Cu2+ was introduced into the solution of LB-CDs, indicating the bond between LB-CDs and Cu2+.

The practical use of this sensing system for Cu2+ detection was investigated using real water samples (i.e., tap and lake water) to verify the use of the developed LB-CDs sensor with respect to ecological Cu2+. The real water specimens were purified by means of plain filtration with 0.22 μm membrane well before the detection. Cu2+ concentration in the practical specimens was determined with the application of the established method. The fluorescent intensity of LB-CDs exhibits a reduction as the specimens have been spiked with Cu2+ standard solutions (Table 3 ). The recoveries of Cu2+ in the spiked specimens range between 95.0% and 105.0%, indicating that the proposed approach exhibits reasonable precision. As indicated by the findings, detecting Cu2+ in ecological waters with the current approach appears to be viable. Furthermore, the plain, affordable, and green preparative strategy is expected to be favorable for application and development.

The probability of application of LB-CDs with respect to cell imaging was examined by the MTT test using A549 cells as the model system (for the empirical details, see the SI). After treatment with several different concentrations of LB-CDs, the A549 cell activity maintained a comparatively high value at all times, even at the concentration of 20 mg mL−1 (Fig. S2). This finding indicates prominent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity toward A549 cells, showing that LB-CDs can effectively replace the traditional semiconductor in the biological area (e.g., bioimaging and bioanalysis). Furthermore, an obvious intracellular fluorescence is observed subsequent to the incubation of LB-CDs with A549 cells. As shown in Fig. 8 , the specimens were observed under bright field, followed by excitation at 405 nm by means of fluorescence microscopy. The consequence indicates that the fluorescent signals stem from the perinuclear areas of the cytosol, which indicates the exceptional cell permeability of LB-CDs into living cells. These findings further validate the fact that the prepared LB-CDs can be used for cell imaging.

In conclusion, we conducted creative synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped CDs on the basis of LB by means of a plain, green, and fast microwave approach without the use of other surface passivation agents. LB-CDs exhibited elevated QY, exceptional aqueous monodispersity, and extraordinary photostability, in addition to being suitable for utilization in solutions with an extensive series of pH values and ionic strengths. LB-CDs also exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity and were utilized as fluorescent probes for the detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 12.8 nM. Investigations of Cu2+ detection in tap and lake water indicated the potential of this sensor for working efficiently in environmental water. Ultimately, the strong fluorescence and low toxicity LB-CDs were applied to cell imaging, which indicated their capability toward diversified uses.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21306063), the Key Research and Development Program of Jiangsu Province (Industry Outlook and Common Key Technologies) (BE2015204), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP51507), and MOE & SAFEA for the 111 Project (B13025) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2018.06.012.
